Storm total snow accumulations across the region generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure retrograding toward New Brunswick from the Atlantic brought heavy snow to much of the region. However...warmer air wrapping around the low allowed the snow to transition to rain across portions of northern and eastern Aroostook county limiting accumulations in that area. The greatest snow totals occurred across the higher terrain of Piscataquis county where storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 24 inches. Storm total snow accumulations across the rest of the region generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches...with localized totals to around 15 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

An ice jam on the Kennebec River at Augusta resulted in minor flooding. The river rose above flood stage early on the 31st and remained above flood stage into the 2nd of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta, (flood stage 12.0 ft). The river remained above flood stage into February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong coastal low pressure produced 3 to 5 inches of rain resulting in county wide flooding of small rivers and streams across portions of southern Maine. Road washouts were numerous with moderate to major damage occurring to roadways. Homes and businesses were also flooded. Total damages across 4 counties in southern Maine amounted to almost a million and a half dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in county wide flooding of small rivers and streams in Knox County Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm system affected the northeastern U.S. from February 23rd through March 2nd.   Areas of surface low pressure rotating around an upper level atmospheric system brought high winds, heavy snow, heavy rain, inland and coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the area during the period.||On Monday, February 22nd, an upper level trough moved east from the Midwest. As the upper level system approached the East Coast on Tuesday, the 23rd, an area of surface low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast and moved northeast to east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 24th.  As this low moved northeast, a very strong and moist southeast flow developed between the low and a large area of high pressure over southeastern Canada.   The initial surface low hooked to the west as it rotated around the upper level low pressure system and gradually weakened.||By the morning of the 25th, a second surface low developed off the east coast and rotated around the upper level system as it intensified rapidly during the day.  Like the first surface low, the pressure gradient between high pressure over southeastern Canada and the intensifying low brought a continuation of the high winds, heavy rain, heavy snow, coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the northeast.   The high winds brought down trees and branches throughout the area and caused widespread power outages and blocked many roadways.  In addition, flooding damaged and/or closed many roads.  Along the coast, the strong east to northeast winds caused tides to surge which led to coastal flooding in the flood-prone areas.  In addition, the strong winds generated large and powerful waves that battered coastal areas and caused coastal damage and erosion.||The upper level low pressure system remained over the northeastern U.S. through the morning of February 28, and then started to move slowly northeast. As it did, a third surface low developed well off the East Coast, moved north, but then rotated northwestward under the influence of the upper level system.  This third low caused the strong northeast winds to continue across much of New England, especially along the coast where the winds continued to produce large waves, and coastal flooding and erosion with each high tide.   ||By the morning of March 2, the upper level system was located southeast of Nova Scotia and moving away from the region.  Winds associated with the surface low gradually diminished during the day although tide levels remained high for several days. ||Precipitation across Maine fell during the period in the form of both rain and snow.      Snowfall across ranged from nothing along the coast to 24 inches in the mountains.  Snowfall amounts were highly dependent on elevation and the higher elevations had significantly more snow than lower elevations which mixed with or changed to rain.||In terms of precipitation, most of the southwest coast and southwest interior zones received from 4 to 7 inches of rain with the mid-coast coastal and interior zones generally receiving 3 to 5 inches of precipitation.  The foothills of Maine generally received from 2 to 4 inches of liquid-equivalent precipitation with the mountains generally receiving 2 to 3 inches of precipitation.||Sustained winds and wind gusts were highly variable across the state  with the strongest winds and gusts generally in coastal areas, especially in the southwestern corner of the state.  The winds and gusts were dependent on elevation and exposure to east and northeast winds.  Based on observed reports, most areas in southwestern Maine likely saw gusts in the 40 to 80 mph range.  The strongest (unofficial) gust reported was 94 mph at Cape Elizabeth.  The strongest official wind gust was 78 mph at Portland, which was a new record for the site.  Official reported peak gusts included 78 mph in Portland, 58 mph at Bar Harbor, 54 mph at Rockland, 52 mph at Wiscasset, 45 mph in Bangor,  43 mph at Lewiston, 40 mph at Augusta, and 31 mph at Fryeburg.  The Sanford observing site reported a 52 mph gust before the site lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing the region produced an isolated strong thunderstorm across northeast Aroostook county during the evening of the 30th which produced large hail.

A minor shortwave with a weak surface reflection moved across New York during the day, and into northern New England late in the afternoon. Although severe weather associated with this system was generally confined to New Hampshire, one isolated cell did hold together as it moved into far southwest Maine producing some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees and wires in the vicinity of Routes 4 and 111 in Alfred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northern portions of Penobscot county during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd in advance of a cold front. The storms produced damaging winds at several locations along with a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were toppled from Shin Pond to Hay Lake by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure approached New England from the west on the 28th and stalled over the region on the 29th. A second area of low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the 29th and intensified as it moved north on the 30th. Low pressure turned out to sea passing through the Gulf of Maine late on the 30th and 31st. The two storms combined to drop 3 to 6 inches of rain across southwest Maine on ground that was already saturated from several storms earlier in the month with generally minor flooding resulting. Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A partial failure of Colcord Dam in Porter caused flooding on two roads which resulted in washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened to 993 mb off the New England coastline during a period of high astronomical tides.  This produced gale force winds along the southwest Maine seacoast.  This system produced 12 foot near-shore waves and a storm surge of over a foot on strong northeasterly gales.  Minor flooding occurred near Commercial Street in Portland.  Roads required plowing of debris in Saco and York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with gale force winds to produce minor coastal flooding in coastal Cumberland County. Flooding occurred near Commercial Street in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the morning of the 25th and deepened as it moved north, reaching southern New England by evening. Ahead of the low, east winds rapidly increased across southern Maine with numerous gusts in excess of 60 mph being reported. Central Maine Power reported 133,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 67 mph was measured at the Portland ASOS.

Heavy rain on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam resulting in minor flooding on the Carrabassett River in North Anson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam resulting in minor flooding on the Carrabassett River, (flood stage 15.0 ft), in North Anson which crested at 16.7 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the morning of the 25th and deepened as it moved north, reaching southern New England by evening. Ahead of the low, east winds rapidly increased across southern Maine with numerous gusts in excess of 60 mph being reported. Central Maine Power reported 133,000 customers without power at the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees on Route 35 in Hollis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major storm system affected the northeastern U.S. from February 23rd through March 2nd.   Areas of surface low pressure rotating around an upper level atmospheric system brought high winds, heavy snow, heavy rain, inland and coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the area during the period.||On Monday, February 22nd, an upper level trough moved east from the Midwest. As the upper level system approached the East Coast on Tuesday, the 23rd, an area of surface low pressure developed off the North Carolina coast and moved northeast to east of the Delmarva Peninsula by the morning of the 24th.  As this low moved northeast, a very strong and moist southeast flow developed between the low and a large area of high pressure over southeastern Canada.   The initial surface low hooked to the west as it rotated around the upper level low pressure system and gradually weakened.||By the morning of the 25th, a second surface low developed off the east coast and rotated around the upper level system as it intensified rapidly during the day.  Like the first surface low, the pressure gradient between high pressure over southeastern Canada and the intensifying low brought a continuation of the high winds, heavy rain, heavy snow, coastal flooding, and coastal erosion to the northeast.   The high winds brought down trees and branches throughout the area and caused widespread power outages and blocked many roadways.  In addition, flooding damaged and/or closed many roads.  Along the coast, the strong east to northeast winds caused tides to surge which led to coastal flooding in the flood-prone areas.  In addition, the strong winds generated large and powerful waves that battered coastal areas and caused coastal damage and erosion.||The upper level low pressure system remained over the northeastern U.S. through the morning of February 28, and then started to move slowly northeast. As it did, a third surface low developed well off the East Coast, moved north, but then rotated northwestward under the influence of the upper level system.  This third low caused the strong northeast winds to continue across much of New England, especially along the coast where the winds continued to produce large waves, and coastal flooding and erosion with each high tide.   ||By the morning of March 2, the upper level system was located southeast of Nova Scotia and moving away from the region.  Winds associated with the surface low gradually diminished during the day although tide levels remained high for several days. ||Precipitation across Maine fell during the period in the form of both rain and snow.      Snowfall across ranged from nothing along the coast to 24 inches in the mountains.  Snowfall amounts were highly dependent on elevation and the higher elevations had significantly more snow than lower elevations which mixed with or changed to rain.||In terms of precipitation, most of the southwest coast and southwest interior zones received from 4 to 7 inches of rain with the mid-coast coastal and interior zones generally receiving 3 to 5 inches of precipitation.  The foothills of Maine generally received from 2 to 4 inches of liquid-equivalent precipitation with the mountains generally receiving 2 to 3 inches of precipitation.||Sustained winds and wind gusts were highly variable across the state  with the strongest winds and gusts generally in coastal areas, especially in the southwestern corner of the state.  The winds and gusts were dependent on elevation and exposure to east and northeast winds.  Based on observed reports, most areas in southwestern Maine likely saw gusts in the 40 to 80 mph range.  The strongest (unofficial) gust reported was 94 mph at Cape Elizabeth.  The strongest official wind gust was 78 mph at Portland, which was a new record for the site.  Official reported peak gusts included 78 mph in Portland, 58 mph at Bar Harbor, 54 mph at Rockland, 52 mph at Wiscasset, 45 mph in Bangor,  43 mph at Lewiston, 40 mph at Augusta, and 31 mph at Fryeburg.  The Sanford observing site reported a 52 mph gust before the site lost power.

Strong coastal low pressure resulted in 5 to 7 inches of rain and caused moderate flooding on the Presumpscot River in Portland and Westbrook. Several homes and businesses were flooded. The river crested over 8.5 feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong coastal low produced 5 to 7 inches of rain in southwest Maine resulting in moderate flooding on the Presumpscot River at Westbrook, (flood stage 15.0 ft). The river crested at 23.55 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong coastal low pressure resulted in 5 to 7 inches of rain and caused moderate flooding on the Presumpscot River in Portland and Westbrook. Several homes and businesses were flooded. The river crested over 8.5 feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused moderate flooding on the Presumpscot River in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of low pressure approached New England from the west on the 28th and stalled over the region on the 29th. A second area of low pressure developed off the mid Atlantic coast on the 29th and intensified as it moved north on the 30th. Low pressure turned out to sea passing through the Gulf of Maine late on the 30th and 31st. The two storms combined to drop 3 to 6 inches of rain across southwest Maine on ground that was already saturated from several storms earlier in the month with generally minor flooding resulting. Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta (flood stage 12.0) which remained above flood stage through the end of the month.

Flooding on some rivers lasted into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused brief minor flooding on the Swift River in Roxbury (flood stage 7.0 ft) which crested at 7.02 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front crossed the region from northwest to southeast on the afternoon of June 20th. A long lived supercell which moved through west central Maine was responsible for numerous wind damage reports and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a large tree and many large branches in Skowhegan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous large tree branches in North Turner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northeast Maine pushed a trailing cold front through the region on the afternoon of May 3rd. Severe thunderstorms associated with this boundary produced several reports of wind damage in central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed 4 trees at a campground in Solon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain which fell during a storm at the end of March caused flooding on the Kennebec River beginning on the 30th of March. The river remained above flood stage through the 1st of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta (flood stage 12.0 ft) which began on the 30th of March and continued through much of the 1st of April. The river crested at 14.3 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northeast Maine pushed a trailing cold front through the region on the afternoon of May 3rd. Severe thunderstorms associated with this boundary produced several reports of wind damage in central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in and around the town of Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over northeast Maine pushed a trailing cold front through the region on the afternoon of May 3rd. Severe thunderstorms associated with this boundary produced several reports of wind damage in central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm uprooted large trees along Belgrade Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm downed trees in Sabattus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm downed trees on Southerland and Williams Roads in Sabattus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain moved across eastern portions of Aroostook county during the evening of the 13th in advance of a cold front. The heavy rains contributed to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with rainfall rates of up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches per hour produced localized flash flooding. Torrents of running water up to a depth of 6 inches were reported covering portions of the Old Washburn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain moved across eastern portions of Aroostook county during the evening of the 13th in advance of a cold front. The heavy rains contributed to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with rainfall rates of up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches per hour produced localized flash flooding. Ansden Brook overflowed its banks flooding Strickland Road and spilling debris across the road. Areas of field flooding and erosion were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain moved across eastern portions of Aroostook county during the evening of the 13th in advance of a cold front. The heavy rains contributed to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled on Grimes Road and Grimes Mill Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a band of thunderstorms which resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rain over a 3 hour period. Many roads were washed out and culverts destroyed due to the heavy rainfall. Several roads in South Thomaston, St. George and Tenants Harbor were closed during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped 3 to 5 inches of rain in 3 hours closing several roads in St. George due to washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough rotating around a large stationary area of low pressure east of Nova Scotia brought a moderate snowfall to much of central Maine.  In the mountains of central Somerset County, the upslope flow enhanced the precipitation and produced from 4 to 10 inches of snow.

Low pressure tracking west of the region drew unseasonably warm moist air north across the region...with record warm temperatures in spots. The warm temperatures rapidly melted the 1 to 2 feet of snow on the ground across central and Downeast portions of the area. The combination of heavy rain and melting snow brought significant flooding to portions of northern and eastern Maine.|Event total rainfall from the 14th through the 15th generally ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches...with localized totals to around 5.00 inches...across much of northern and eastern Maine. The heaviest rains fell across Washington county where thunderstorms helped increase event total rainfall to 3.00 to 6.00 inches...with localized totals of 8.00 to 9.00 inches.|Flooding extensively damaged roads and bridges across the entire region. Numerous roads were closed and impasable during the event due to water of varying depths covering the roads. In the wake of the flooding...many roads remained closed due to damage. Damage ranged from complete washouts and collapses...to severe shoulder erosion...to damaged or destroyed culverts.|Strong south to southeast winds occurred in advance of the low during the 13th. Winds were sustained at speeds of 30 to 40 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The strongest wind gusts occurred near the coast with the onshore winds. A peak wind gust of 70 mph was reported at Lubec in coastal Washington county. The winds toppled trees and power lines...with the most extensive damage occurring near the coast. Several thousand customers lost power for varying amounts of time during the event...mostly across Downeast areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds toppled trees and power lines...leading to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure crossing the Gulf of Maine brought blizzard conditions to all of northern and eastern Maine. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common across the entire region...with gusts up to 60 mph in spots. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across much of the region...with localized totals of 15 to 18 inches. The strong winds contributed to power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with localized totals to around 12 inches. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph...with occasional gusts to 60 mph...produced extensive blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities to near zero.

At the peak of the event numerous roads across the entire county were closed due to flooding. A vehicle in both Baring and Machias fell into sinkholes where water had undercut the roads...no injuries were reported in either incident. Flood damage was particularly extensive in the area from Machias...to Dennysville to Calais. The Moosehorn National Wildlife Refuge reported extensive damage to roads and other infrastructure due to the flooding. Numerous basements were also flooded across the county. The monetary damage estimate represents a total for the entire county for the entire event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Explosive intensification of low pressure in the Gulf of Maine during the early morning hours of November 8th resulted in high winds across much of coastal and southern interior Maine. Winds gusted from 35 to 63 mph. Central Maine Power Company reported 61,445 customers without power during the height of the storm. Trees, telephone poles and wires were downed over much of the area during the period of peak winds. More than 40 poles snapped during the storm in Lincoln County alone.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed many trees and caused numerous power outages throughout the area.  Central Maine Power reported more the 8700 power outages in the Sagadahoc County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure developed in the Gulf States and intensified as it moved from the coast of the Carolinas on the morning of the 26th, to Cape Cod on the morning of the 27th, and to northeastern Nova Scotia by the morning of the 28th.  Heavy snow fell across much of western and central Maine with amounts generally totaling about 6 to 12 inches with locally higher amounts.  Rangeley reported 23.5 inches from the storm.  In addition to heavy snow, the intense storm brought and blizzard to near blizzard conditions across the state.  Blizzard conditions were reported at the airport in Augusta from about 10:15 am to 4:30 pm on the 27th.  Central Maine Power reported that about 3200 customers lost electrical service during the storm.

Flooding closed several roads...with some roads remaining closed after the flooding subsided due to damage. The monetary damage estimate represents a total for the entire county for the entire event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure crossing the Gulf of Maine brought blizzard conditions to all of northern and eastern Maine. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common across the entire region...with gusts up to 60 mph in spots. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 12 inches across much of the region...with localized totals of 15 to 18 inches. The strong winds contributed to power outages across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 16 inches. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph...with occasional gusts to 60 mph...produced extensive blowing and drifting snow reducing visibilities to near zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure developed in the Gulf States and intensified as it moved from the coast of the Carolinas on the morning of the 26th, to Cape Cod on the morning of the 27th, and to northeastern Nova Scotia by the morning of the 28th.  Heavy snow fell across much of western and central Maine with amounts generally totaling about 6 to 12 inches with locally higher amounts.  Rangeley reported 23.5 inches from the storm.  In addition to heavy snow, the intense storm brought and blizzard to near blizzard conditions across the state.  Blizzard conditions were reported at the airport in Augusta from about 10:15 am to 4:30 pm on the 27th.  Central Maine Power reported that about 3200 customers lost electrical service during the storm.

Low pressure intensifying south of Nova Scotia during the 1st retrogressed toward the Maine coast during the 2nd bringing significant snow to much of northern and eastern Maine. Snow expanded northwestward across the region through the morning and early afternoon hours of the 2nd. The snow then continued...heavy at times...through the remainder of the 2nd before ending during the early morning hours of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations of 12 to 18 inches were common across much of Downeast Maine...with 7 to 12 inch storm totals across much of northern Maine.  This snow was in addition to 2 to 4 inches of accumulation which had occurred Downeast from New Years Eve into the 1st. Winds also increased while the intensifying low retrogressed toward the coast. Near blizzard conditions developed across portions of Washington and Hancock counties during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Occasional wind gusts in excess of 35 to 40 mph occurred in these areas contributing to reduced visibilities along with significant blowing and drifting snow. |The combination of extremely high astronomical tides and a 1 to 2 foot storm surge from the approaching low caused water to back up into the Penobscot River...which then backed up into the Kenduskeag Stream. This caused the Kenduskeag Stream to overflow its banks during the time of high tide from the late morning into the early afternoon of the 2nd...producing minor flooding along the stream in downtown Bangor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong damaging south to southeast winds occurred during the evening of the 25th with a low level jet which crossed the region in advance of an occluded front. The low level jet moved east of the region during the early morning hours of the 26th. The strong winds snapped branches and toppled numerous trees. Falling branches and trees brought down many power lines...contributing to power outages. In excess of 5000 customers lost power at various times during the event...with the greatest impacts along the Downeast coast and in the Bangor/Brewer area. In Brewer...the winds partially tore the roof from the New Stable Inn forcing an evacuation of the building. Several roads across Downeast Maine were blocked and closed due to fallen trees and power lines. A peak wind gust of 68 mph was reported at both Lubec and West Quoddy in coastal Washington county...with 50 to 60 mph wind gusts common along the entire Downeast coast. |Heavy rains also occurred across portions of the region in advance of the occlusion. Storm total rainfall of 1.50 to 2.50 inches occurred across portions of the central highlands during the 25th. The combination of runoff from the rain and melting snow led to rising water levels on rivers and streams. The rising water levels caused ice to  begin to move and jam during the late morning of the 26th. Ice jam flooding affected portions of southern Piscataquis county and a small portion of central Penobscot county through the afternoon of the 26th into the morning of the 27th. Most of the flooding was minor...mainly affecting low lying areas along rivers and streams. However...several roads were covered with water at times with some roads damaged. River and stream levels fluctuated through the remainder of the 26th into the 27th while ice continued to move and re-jam before stabilizing during the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 50 to 60 mph snapped branches and toppled trees across the region. The falling branches and trees brought down power lines which contributed to numerous power outages. In Brewer...the winds partially tore the roof from the New Stable Inn which had to be evacuated due to the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensifying south of Nova Scotia during the 1st retrogressed toward the Maine coast during the 2nd bringing significant snow to much of northern and eastern Maine. Snow expanded northwestward across the region through the morning and early afternoon hours of the 2nd. The snow then continued...heavy at times...through the remainder of the 2nd before ending during the early morning hours of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations of 12 to 18 inches were common across much of Downeast Maine...with 7 to 12 inch storm totals across much of northern Maine.  This snow was in addition to 2 to 4 inches of accumulation which had occurred Downeast from New Years Eve into the 1st. Winds also increased while the intensifying low retrogressed toward the coast. Near blizzard conditions developed across portions of Washington and Hancock counties during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd. Occasional wind gusts in excess of 35 to 40 mph occurred in these areas contributing to reduced visibilities along with significant blowing and drifting snow. |The combination of extremely high astronomical tides and a 1 to 2 foot storm surge from the approaching low caused water to back up into the Penobscot River...which then backed up into the Kenduskeag Stream. This caused the Kenduskeag Stream to overflow its banks during the time of high tide from the late morning into the early afternoon of the 2nd...producing minor flooding along the stream in downtown Bangor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 18 inches. Winds occasionally gusting in excess of 35 to 40 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times during the evening of the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam to form resulting in minor flooding on the Saco River in Fryeburg. The Route 113 bridge in Fryeburg was closed for a time due to an ice jam that formed just upstream.

A very tight pressure gradient ahead of an approaching frontal system resulted in many reports of trees and wires down across southern Maine. Gusts as high as 63 mph were measured during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed a tree on Shaws Ridge Road in Sanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between intense low pressure across southern New England and high pressure to the north produced strong east winds across Downeast Maine. The strongest winds occurred from the evening of the 25th into the early morning hours of the 26th. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred across interior Downeast areas...with 60 to 70 mph gusts along the coast. |The combination of strong winds and wet ground led to thousands of trees being uprooted or snapped across the region. The most extensive tree damage was reported across southwest portions of Hancock county including Deer Isle...Stonington...Brooklin...Sedgwick...Blue Hill and Mount Desert Island. The falling trees brought down many power lines resulting in thousands of customers loosing power. The damage to the power infrastructure was extensive enough that in some locations power was not restored for several days. The falling trees also blocked many roads. In one instance...a tree fell on a parked unoccupied vehicle producing significant damage.|The low became nearly stationary across southern New England while several areas of moisture rotated around the low...producing areas of heavy snow across portions of northern Maine. The first round of heavy snow occurred from the overnight hours of the 24th into the 25th when 7 to 11 inches of accumulation were reported across  northern portions of Somerset and Piscataquis counties. A second interval of heavy snow occurred during the 26th affecting portions of northern Penobscot and northwest Aroostook counties. During this second event....7 to 12 inches of accumulation were reported across northwest Aroostook county with 5 to 9 inches across portions of northern Penobscot county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulation ranged from 7 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm was slowly weakening over southern New England on the morning of February 26th.  Long period swells were slow to subside and some minor flooding occurred in Portland and Saco at the time of high tide.  In Saco, flooding shut down Water and Surf streets. In Portland Harbor an astronomical high tide of 10.47 ft. combined with a storm surge of 1.92 ft. for a storm tide of 12.12 ft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense storm was slowly weakening over southern New England on the morning of February 26th.  Long period swells were slow to subside and some minor flooding occurred in Saco at the time of high tide shutting down Water and Surf streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm, (971 mb), retrograded towards the New England coastline on the 25th of February.  This storm produced hurricane force winds with peak wind gusts of 91 mph at the Isles of Shoals.  This produced near-shore waves as high as 32 feet and the seventeenth largest storm surge ever for Portland at 2.7 feet.  Significant splash-over occurred which produced flooding in low lying areas such as Saco, Kennebunk and  Kennebunkport.  In Saco, near-shore roads were closed due to flooding. The storm tide reached 11.58 ft. with seas of 23 ft. at the time of high tide in Portland Harbor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system retrograding toward the New England coast produced hurricane force winds and waves of 22 to 32 ft. Significant splash-over at the time of high tide produced coastal flooding in coastal Cumberland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure remained over the southern New England coastline for several days.  This system continued to produce a modest storm surge and long period swells.  Minor flooding occurred during the morning high tide along city streets in Portland in and around the wharf area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure over southern New England produced a modest storm surge and long period swell during high tide on the morning of the 27th.  Minor flooding occurred along city streets in Portland in and around the wharf area.

Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches across much of the area.

Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam on the Kennebec River in Augusta. This ice jam eventually moved downstream to Hallowell. Businesses in Augusta and Hallowell suffered minor damage due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam to form on the Kennebec River in Augusta (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 18.09 ft.

An ice jam on the Kennebec River at Augusta result in minor flooding. The river rose above flood stage early on the 31st of January and remained above flood stage until the evening of the 2nd of February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Kennebec River at Augusta, (flood stage 12.0 ft), resulted in minor flooding. The river crested at 13.33 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved northeast out of the Ohio River Valley spreading heavy rain and warm temperatures into the region. Temperatures warmed into the 50s with 3 to 5 inches of rainfall. The combination of heavy rain and melting snow caused minor flooding on many rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and melting snow caused minor flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs) which crested at 65,500 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure tracked north along the East Coast on the 23rd and then turned northeast, passing through the Gulf of Maine on the 24th. A tropical connection helped pump abundant moisture northward with rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches falling on already saturated ground. In addition, snow melt in the mountains contributed to rises on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Presumpscot River in Westbrook (flood Stage 15.0 ft) which crested at 16.7 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor���easter slowly weakened off the southern New England coast on the evening of the 30th.  Moderately large ocean waves combined with the highest astronomical tide of the month to produce beach erosion, splash-over and coastal flooding.  In Portland, the water level approached Commercial Street.  Splash-over occurred with flooding on Water Street in Saco with rocks and seaweed thrown on to Kennebunk beach.  Coastal flooding and splash-over deposited debris on Long Beach and Shore Road in York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomical high tide combined with a weakening Nor'easter to produce coastal flooding in coastal Cumberland County. The storm tide in Portland Harbor topped out at 12.60 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing storm retrograded into the Gulf of Maine from the Atlantic early on March 1st.  This system produced 9 to 16 foot near-shore waves and a storm surge of over a foot on strong northerly gales.  Minor flooding occurred along the city streets in Portland in and around the wharf area.  Splash-over was significant enough on Water Street in Saco to close the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A developing storm retrograded into the Gulf of Maine from the Atlantic producing seas of 9 to 16 ft. and a storm surge of over a foot. Minor flooding occurred along city streets in Portland in and around the wharf area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the Carolinas on March 13 and moved northeast spreading heavy rain over New England. Rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to 9 inches in southwest Maine which caused serious flooding. York County was especially hard hit with a million dollars worth of damage due to flooding of small rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on the Presumpscot River in Westbrook (flood stage 15.0 ft) which crested at 18.5 ft.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread flooding of small rivers and streams throughout York County. Estimated damages to property and infrastructure topped 1 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam on the Kennebec River in Augusta. This ice jam eventually moved downstream to Hallowell. Businesses in Augusta and Hallowell suffered minor damage due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, on the order of 2 inches, combined with melting snow to cause an ice jam to form on the Kennebec River at Hallowell.

Low pressure over northeast Maine pushed a trailing cold front through the region on the afternoon of May 3rd. Severe thunderstorms associated with this boundary produced several reports of wind damage in central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm uprooted dozens of trees in and around Pittsfield. Central Maine Power estimated 11,000 customers were left without power by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area on the afternoon of May 6th. Thunderstorms, embedded in a strong wind field, moved east at 35 mph and produced wind damage in northwestern Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and power lines 7 miles west of Rangeley. A wind gust of 48 mph was recorded at the airport in Rangeley as the storm moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds crossed Downeast Maine during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 3rd in advance of a cold front. Numerous reports of toppled and snapped trees...along with downed power lines...were received. Around 10,000 customers lost power for varying lengths of time during the event. Minor structural damage to some roofs also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled on to power lines along Junior Ralph Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph...leading to power outages.

Numerous reports of toppled or snapped trees...along with downed power lines...were received. Minor structural damage was also reported at several locations. The driver of a vehicle near Houlton was injured when a tree was blown on to the car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient between low pressure approaching from the west and high pressure to the east produced strong south winds across portions of Downeast Maine during the afternoon and evening hours of the 29th. The winds snapped branches and the combination of wind and wet ground led to trees being uprooted. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines leading to several thousand customers losing power. The most extensive power outages occurred in the Blue Hill and Brooklin areas of Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph toppled trees and snapped branches across the region. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines which contributed to power outages. The most extensive tree damage was reported in the towns of Orono and Hampden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of heavy rain and melting snow caused the Mattawamkeag River at Mattawamkeag to rise slightly above flood stage. The river gradually rose above flood stage during the early morning hours of the 1st. The river crested just over a half foot above flood stage during the afternoon of the 2nd. The river slowly fell thereafter...falling below flood stage during the morning of the 5th. The flooding was minor...affecting only low lying wooded areas along the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mattawamkeag River at Mattawamkeag crested just over a half foot above flood stage. The flooding was minor...affecting only low lying wooded areas along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure retrogressing toward Nova Scotia produced high winds across Downeast Maine during the morning of the 1st. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common across Downeast areas...particularly near the coast. The winds snapped branches and the combination of winds and wet ground led to trees being uprooted. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines which led to power outages. The power outages were most extensive along the Downeast coast. Fallen trees also blocked roads in some towns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph toppled trees and snapped branches across the region. The falling trees and branches brought down power lines contributing to power outages. The most extensive tree damage was reported in the towns of Meddybemps and Baring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northern portions of Penobscot county during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd in advance of a cold front. The storms produced damaging winds at several locations along with a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were snapped or uprooted along Grand Lake Road...west of Huber Road...by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northern portions of Penobscot county during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd in advance of a cold front. The storms produced damaging winds at several locations along with a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado snapped or uprooted numerous trees in a remote portion of northern Penobscot county near Shin Pond. Some trees were snapped near their base while the majority were snapped further up the trunk...with several of the trees around 20 inches in diameter and 100 feet tall. Based on the storm survey...it was determined that the tornadic circulation generally remained just above the surface along much of its path...affecting mostly the tree tops.  The most extensive tree damage occurred along...and just north of...Grand Lake Road between Scraggly Lake Road and Flemming Brook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northern portions of Penobscot county during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 2nd in advance of a cold front. The storms produced damaging winds at several locations along with a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was partially toppled on to powerlines by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The tree hung on the powerlines and caught fire. Two sisters and their stepfather went outside to look at the buring tree. While they were standing there...the tree completely fell bringing down the powerlines. The powerlines fell across the girls legs burning them. The stepfather received arm and shoulder injuries after being thrown by a jolt of electricity while attempting to remove the powerline from the girls. All three were transported to the hospital. The injuries were determined to be indirect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in Newfield on the west side of Patterson Road and moved east into Limerick crossing Patterson and Foss roads before lifting off the ground.  The E-F1 tornado was estimated to have maximum winds of 90 mph and cut a path almost 2 miles in length and up to 200 yards wide.  Hundreds of trees were snapped or uprooted by the tornado.  In addition, the tornado damaged several homes and buildings in the area.  Sections of the metal roof of one home were found about 0.2 miles from the house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home on Ash Point Road and ignited a fire that did extensive damage to the interior of the home.  The lightning struck the roof of the home and started a fire in an upstairs closet before spreading.  No one was injured in the blaze, but damage was estimated at $70,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail along with 3 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just west of East Webster Road in Buxton and then moved east into Gorham.  The tornado produced a discontinuous path of damage through Gorham before lifting off the ground near the intersection of Routes 237 and 25.  The tornado snapped or uprooted hundreds of trees along of its track.  The most significant damage was observed near Route 202 where maximum winds were estimated at near 90 mph. The tornado was on the ground for about 5 miles, had a maximum width of about 350 yards, and was classified as an E-F1 based on the estimated wind speed.  The tornado downed trees unto power lines, homes. and cars in the area and caused numerous power outages.  In one instance, farm trailers were moved 50 to 75 feet by the tornado and sheds were destroyed. In Gorham...damage was noted along Finn Parker and Wood Roads...Lovers Lane...Kimball Road...Wentworth Drive...and Lawn and Libby Roads as well as Route 237.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail along with 3 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds from a severe thunderstorm brought down numerous trees in an area from Phippsburg to Georgetown.   In Phippsburg the most severe damage occurred in and around Parker Head Road and Captain John Parker Road where scattered trees were uprooted or snapped by the storm.  Winds were estimated to have gusted between 60 and 70 mph in the area.  ||In Georgetown, the severe thunderstorm line winds brought down more than a hundred of trees and damaged one vehicle in the area around Bay Point and Marrtown Roads.  Although some of the damage was scattered...an area just east of Bay Point Road was particularly hard hit.  Winds were estimated to have gusted up to 80 mph in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail along with 3 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down between Back and Walnut Hill Roads in Shapleigh and moved east into Alfred before lifting off the ground on the east side of Back Road in Alfred.  The E-F1 tornado was estimated to have maximum winds of 90 mph and cut a path about 5.4 miles in length and up to 200 yards wide.  Damage from the tornado was observed across or near Stone Haven Hill Road and Walnut Hill Road in Shapleigh...and Gebung, Bracket Hill, Gore, Rt 202, Cranberry Meadows and Back Roads in Alfred. The tornado downed hundreds of trees along its path and falling trees knocked down utility lines and snapped power poles in the path.  Several structures were also damaged by falling trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled on West Presque Isle Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a band of thunderstorms which resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rain over a 3 hour period. Many roads were washed out and culverts destroyed due to the heavy rainfall. Several roads in South Thomaston, St. George and Tenants Harbor were closed during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped 3 to 5 inches of rain over a 3 hour period in South Thomaston closing several roads due to washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure crossing southern Canada pushed a trailing cold front toward northern New England during the late afternoon and evening. Ahead of the front, thunderstorm activity quickly turned severe with numerous supercells producing damaging winds and large hail along with 3 tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced straight line wind damage in an area just north of Jenkins Road in Saco where about one hundred trees were snapped or uprooted by the wind. Timber in the area had been recently harvested...leaving the remaining trees in and immediately surrounding the harvested area more susceptible to wind damage.  One home in the area sustained minor damage from the wind. Winds were estimated to have gusted between 60 and 70 mph in the damaged area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain moved across eastern portions of Aroostook county during the evening of the 13th in advance of a cold front. The heavy rains contributed to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with rainfall rates of up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches per hour produced localized flash flooding around the Caribou area. The Spring Brook overflowed its banks flooding portions of Dow Siding Road. Water and debris were reported across  River Road near the intersection of Route 161. A minor washout was also reported on Fenderson Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through northern New England produced scattered convection across northern and central Maine. A few of these thunderstorm cells became severe and produced mainly wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Main Street in Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced flash flooding across portions of extreme northeast Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms containing very heavy rain produced up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain in several hours. The heavy rains contributed to flash flooding around the towns of Van Buren and Keegan. Water depths of over a foot covered numerous portions of Route 1 around the Van Buren area. The flood waters spilled into shops in downtown Van Buren to depths of over a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced flash flooding across portions of extreme northeast Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms containing very heavy rain produced up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain in several hours. The heavy rains contributed to flash flooding around the town of Eagle Lake. Rapidly flowing water estimated at up to 3 feet deep severely eroded a portion of a yard and driveway. A large stormwater culvert was also overtopped. Flood waters filled the basement of a house to a depth of 5 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced flash flooding across portions of extreme northeast Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms containing very heavy rain produced up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain in several hours. The heavy rains contributed to flash flooding which washed out a portion of a railbed causing a shutdown of the railroad line between Van Buren and Grand Isle. Damages to the railbed were estimated at up to $75,000 dollars. The flash flooding also caused $5,000 dollars of damage to a farm due to a flooded garage...a road washout...destroyed fencing and half an acre of ruined crops on the property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced flash flooding across portions of extreme northeast Aroostook county during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms containing very heavy rain produced up to 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain in several hours. The heavy rains contributed to flash flooding on the Sanford Stream which completely washed out a portion of Lake Road. Water depths in excess of 4 feet were reported over the road at the washout point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain moved across eastern portions of Aroostook county during the evening of the 13th in advance of a cold front. The heavy rains contributed to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were toppled on Gardiner Creek Road by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and very heavy rain moved across eastern portions of Aroostook county during the evening of the 13th in advance of a cold front. The heavy rains contributed to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled...uprooted or snapped along Route 164 between Washburn and Crouseville by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Earl made landfall on the east coast of Nova Scotia during the mid morning of the 4th. The minimum surface pressure across the region with the storm was 989 mb. The storm brought sustained winds of 15 to 25 mph to the immediate Downeast coast during the early morning hours of the 4th while tracking east of the region. Wind gusts near the immediate coast generally ranged from 30 to 40 mph. A peak wind gust of 41 mph was reported at West Quoddy. There were no reports of wind related damage. Storm surge was not a factor.|The storm also brought heavy rain to portions of the region. Rainfall totals of 1.50 to 3.00 inches were common across much of the region...with localized totals of 4.00 to 5.00 inches. However...the heavy rains fell after several weeks of dry conditions which had led to unusually low water levels on area rivers and brooks. Thus...although the heavy rain caused elevated water levels on small brooks and rivers...no river or brook flooding was reported. Minor street flooding was reported in the town of Machias...with one street temporarily closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Earl tracked east of the region during the early morning hours of the 4th...eventually making landfall on the east coast of Nova Scotia mid morning. However...the rains occurred after several weeks of dry conditions which had led to unusually low water levels. Thus...although the heavy rain caused elevated water levels on brooks and rivers...no flooding was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of strong winds moved north across the region in advance of rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking toward the Gulf of Maine. The strongest winds occurred through the morning and afternoon hours of the 15th. The winds snapped branches and toppled numerous trees across the central highlands and Downeast areas. The falling trees and branches helped bring down power lines which contributed to numerous power outages. The most extensive power outages occurred across Washington and Hancock counties where at one point around 8000 customers were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds snapped branches and toppled trees. The snapped branches and falling trees brought down power lines which contributed to numerous power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet in advance of a cold front brought damaging south to southeast winds to portions of the central highlands and Downeast areas. The strongest winds occurred during the early morning hours of the 1st. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees which contributed to downed power lines and power outages. At one point nearly 3500 customers were without power...mostly across Hancock county along with southern portions of Penobscot county. A line of low topped convection along the cold front helped mix the strong winds just above the ground to the surface producing isolated convective wind damage at the town of Crystal in Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong damaging winds toppled trees and snapped branches around the towns of Sedgwick...Blue Hill...Ellsworth...Bar Harbor and Gouldsboro. The falling trees and snapped branches helped bring down power lines which contributed to power outages. At one point...around 1800 customers were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong east to southeast winds occurred along the Downeast coast in advance of low pressure lifting northward along an occluded front. The strongest winds occurred during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 17th. The strong winds snapped branches and toppled trees which brought down power lines contributing to power outages. At one point...around 750 customers were without power in Hancock County mostly around  the towns of Brooklin and Blue Hill. A small area of low topped convection developed along the occlusion during the early evening hours. Winds with this convection snapped and toppled trees at several locations around the town of Haynesville in southeast Aroostook County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds snapped branches and toppled trees. The falling branches and trees brought down power lines which contributed to power outages. The strong winds tore roof tiles from houses in Easport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds occurred across central and coastal portions of Washington county...to the east of low pressure lifting north across Maine. The strongest winds occurred during the morning of the 5th. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees which brought down power lines contributing to power outages. At one point...around 5500 customers were without power. Power outages were particularly extensive in the towns of Eastport...Princeton...Wesley...Alexander...Crawford...Addison...Calais and Beals. The strong onshore winds also combined with an astronomical high tide to produce minor coastal flooding at the town of Machias around the time of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds snapped branches and toppled trees. The falling branches and trees brought down power lines which contributed to power outages. A 57 mph wind gust was reported at West Quoddy with a 51 mph wind gust reported on the Schoodic Peninsula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moving across southern Canada provided broad scale ascent and instability which led to the development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. Strong winds aloft (>50KT at 700mb) provided enough shear for organization and primarily a wind threat. There were numerous reports of wind damage from bow echoes and one supercell that moved through York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Biddeford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave approaching from the northwest and a prefrontal trough were the catalyst for severe thunderstorms and some super cells across northern New England  on the afternoon of June 5th. As early morning convection associated with a warm front, some of which was severe, moved east of the area, strong surface heating and good low level shear combined to produce several long lived thunderstorms.  A super cell, which earlier dropped an EF0 tornado in northern New Hampshire, moved into southern Oxford county producing 1 to 2 inch hail and spawning an EF1 tornado. The tornado first touched down in the town of Paris and moved east through portions of Oxford and Hebron. This storm remained severe as it crossed central Maine producing large hail, damaging winds and several reports of funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud associated with a severe thunderstorm was reported in the town of Minot.

A super cell, which earlier dropped an EF0 tornado in northern New Hampshire, moved into southern Oxford county producing 1 to 2 inch hail and spawning an EF1 tornado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the town of Paris and traveled through parts of the towns of Oxford and Hebron. The tornado touched down near the intersection of Hebron Road and Inman Road in Paris...crossed Hebron Road in Oxford...and then lifted off the ground near Merrill Hill Road in Hebron. The greatest damage occurred near where it crossed Hebron Road in Oxford where maximum winds were estimated at near 90 mph...causing the tornado to be rated an E-F1 on the enhanced Fujita Scale. The tornado was on the ground for about 4.20 miles and had a maximum width of about 300 yards. ||Along its path...the tornado uprooted or snapped hundreds of trees as it traveled through mostly wooded areas. The tornado also flipped over a camper trailer...and falling trees damaged several structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moving across southern Canada provided broad scale ascent and instability which led to the development of thunderstorms in the afternoon. Strong winds aloft (>50KT at 700mb) provided enough shear for organization and primarily a wind threat. There were numerous reports of wind damage from bow echoes and one supercell that moved through York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed several trees in Eliot.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm winds damaged sections of the town of york where winds were estimated to have gusted between 80 and 90 mph at around 7 pm Thursday evening, June 24.  Several thousand large trees were snapped or uprooted by the storm and between 40 and 50 utility poles were snapped by the falling trees. In addition, houses, buildings and vehicles were damaged by falling trees and branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong short wave approaching from the northwest and a prefrontal trough were the catalyst for severe thunderstorms and some super cells across northern New England  on the afternoon of June 5th. As early morning convection associated with a warm front, some of which was severe, moved east of the area, strong surface heating and good low level shear combined to produce several long lived thunderstorms.  A super cell, which earlier dropped an EF0 tornado in northern New Hampshire, moved into southern Oxford county producing 1 to 2 inch hail and spawning an EF1 tornado. The tornado first touched down in the town of Paris and moved east through portions of Oxford and Hebron. This storm remained severe as it crossed central Maine producing large hail, damaging winds and several reports of funnel clouds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced .75 inch hail in Litchfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds crossed Downeast Maine during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 3rd in advance of a cold front. Numerous reports of toppled and snapped trees...along with downed power lines...were received. Around 10,000 customers lost power for varying lengths of time during the event. Minor structural damage to some roofs also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front crossed the region from northwest to southeast on the afternoon of June 20th. A long lived supercell which moved through west central Maine was responsible for numerous wind damage reports and some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees just north of Greenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure tracked north along the East Coast on the 23rd and then turned northeast, passing through the Gulf of Maine on the 24th. A tropical connection helped pump abundant moisture northward with rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches falling on already saturated ground. In addition, snow melt in the mountains contributed to rises on some rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread flooding of small rivers and streams throughout York County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a band of thunderstorms which resulted in 3 to 5 inches of rain over a 3 hour period. Many roads were washed out and culverts destroyed due to the heavy rainfall. Several roads in South Thomaston, St. George and Tenants Harbor were closed during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped 3 to 5 inches of rain over a 3 hour period in Tenants Harbor closing several roads due to washouts.
